news_summary: Recent health advances highlight natural treatments targeting rheumatoid arthritis, insights into viral polymerase inhibition, the importance of gut health, and the benefits of short HIIT sessions in mitigating sleep debt’s metabolic effects.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées en matière de santé mettent en avant les traitements naturels ciblant la polyarthrite rhumatoïde, les découvertes concernant l'inhibition de la polymérase virale, l'importance de la santé intestinale et les bienfaits des séances courtes d'entraînement par intervalles à haute intensité (HIIT) pour atténuer les effets métaboliques du manque de sommeil.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud ponen de relieve los tratamientos naturales para la artritis reumatoide, los conocimientos sobre la inhibición de la polimerasa viral, la importancia de la salud intestinal y los beneficios de las sesiones cortas de HIIT para mitigar los efectos metabólicos del déficit de sueño.

articles.title: Natural compound may fight rheumatoid arthritis at its source
articles.summary: A plant-derived compound reduced swelling, inflammation, and joint damage in rats with rheumatoid arthritis. It also helped rebalance immune activity and improve the way certain fats were processed in the body. Researchers say the findings could lead to a new approach for treating the disease.
articles.content:
A natural compound called obakulactone (OL) may offer a new way to treat rheumatoid arthritis (RA), according to a study published in Engineering. OL is a tetracyclic triterpenoid isolated from Phellodendri cortex. Researchers found that it reduced signs of arthritis by promoting the breakdown of acyl coenzyme A thioesterase 1 (ACOT1) through the ubiquitin‒proteasome pathway and by restoring the balance of unsaturated fatty acids.

The findings help explain how OL acts on rheumatoid arthritis at the molecular level. They also identify ACOT1 as a possible new drug target and suggest that correcting disrupted fatty acid metabolism could become a useful strategy for treating RA.

Obakulactone Reduced Joint Swelling and Damage

Researchers tested OL in rats with rheumatoid arthritis triggered by complete Freund's adjuvant (CFA). The animals received low (50 mg·kg-1·d-1), medium (100 mg·kg-1·d-1), and high (200 mg·kg-1·d-1) doses of OL for 21 days.

Treatment significantly reduced swelling in the joints. It also helped restore the normal structure of cartilage and the synovium, the tissue that lines the inside of joints. In addition, OL improved abnormal changes in immune organs including the thymus and spleen.

The compound also altered immune activity within the joints. It reduced the unusually high levels of CD3+ T cells and CD68+ macrophages. At the same time, it shifted macrophages away from the proinflammatory M1 (CD86) state and toward the anti-inflammatory M2 (CD206) state. OL also limited the development of CD4+ T cells into inflammation-promoting Th17 cells.

Blood tests showed that OL lowered several inflammatory molecules in a dose-dependent manner. These included IL-1β, IL-6, IL-17, and TNF-α. The treatment also reduced rheumatoid arthritis markers including RF, CCP-Ab, CRP, and MMP-3.

Restoring Disrupted Fatty Acid Metabolism

The researchers used multiomics techniques, including metabolomics, MALDI mass spectrometry imaging, and proteomics, to examine how OL affected biological processes throughout the body.

Their analysis showed that rheumatoid arthritis had disrupted the production and metabolism of several unsaturated fatty acids. OL helped correct these abnormalities, including changes involving arachidonic acid, linoleic acid, and α-linolenic acid.

Laboratory experiments also examined the effects of OL on RA synovial fibroblasts (SFs). These cells can grow excessively in rheumatoid arthritis and contribute to inflammation, thickening of joint tissue, and damage to cartilage and bone.

OL slowed the growth of the abnormal fibroblasts, encouraged them to undergo apoptosis, and reduced their release of inflammatory cytokines.

ACOT1 Identified as a Direct Target

A series of tests, including cellular thermal shift assays, microscale thermophoresis, and surface plasmon resonance experiments, showed that OL binds directly to ACOT1.

The researchers measured a dissociation constant (Kd) of (6.18 ± 0.26) μmol·L-1 (analyzed by microscale thermophoresis (MST)) and (6.34 ± 0.38) μmol·L-1 (analyzed by surface plasmon resonance (SPR)).

OL increased the ubiquitination-mediated proteasomal degradation of ACOT1. In this process, cells attach molecular tags to the protein and send it to the proteasome, the cellular machinery responsible for breaking down unwanted proteins.

Reducing ACOT1 also lowered levels of the downstream protein stearoyl-CoA desaturase-1 (SCD1). This, in turn, limited activation of the Janus kinase (JAK)-signal transducer and activator of transcription (STAT) and phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K)-protein kinase B (AKT) signaling pathways.

These pathways help regulate cell survival, growth, inflammation, and fibrosis. By suppressing their activity, OL reduced inflammatory and fibrotic changes in SFs.

Additional rescue experiments and studies using inhibitors supported the proposed mechanism. The results indicated that OL produced its anti-inflammatory, antiproliferative, and proapoptotic effects by targeting ACOT1, regulating the arachidonic acid pathway, and influencing the downstream JAK-STAT/PI3K-AKT signaling pathways.

A Potential New Rheumatoid Arthritis Strategy

RA is a chronic systemic autoimmune disease that affects approximately 1% of people worldwide. It occurs when the immune system mistakenly attacks healthy joint tissue, causing pain, swelling, stiffness, and progressive damage. Existing treatments do not work equally well for everyone and can sometimes cause serious adverse effects.

The new findings provide preclinical evidence that OL could serve as a potential therapeutic compound for rheumatoid arthritis. They also highlight ACOT1 and unsaturated fatty acid metabolism as promising targets for future drug development.

Because the research was conducted in rats and isolated cells, further studies will be needed to determine whether OL is safe and effective in humans.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a natural compound, obakulactone, that shows promising therapeutic effects against rheumatoid arthritis in preclinical studies. It details the compound's mechanism, its impact on inflammation and immune regulation, and identifies new drug targets, suggesting potential for broad clinical benefits in treating a chronic autoimmune disease affecting millions worldwide. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful advancement in health research with hopeful implications.
articles.personality_title: Natural compound shows promise in reducing rheumatoid arthritis symptoms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a disease where the body’s immune system attacks the joints, causing swelling, pain, and damage. Current treatments don’t always work well for everyone and can have side effects.

**What happened** – Scientists studied a natural compound called obakulactone (OL), found in a plant, to see if it could help with RA. They gave different doses of OL to rats with arthritis for 21 days. The treatment reduced joint swelling, improved damaged joint tissues, and balanced immune cells that cause inflammation. OL also helped fix problems with certain fats in the body that are linked to RA. The researchers found that OL works by targeting a protein called ACOT1, which plays a role in the disease.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows a new way to treat RA by focusing on fat metabolism and a specific protein. OL not only lowered inflammation but also helped stop harmful joint cell growth. This could lead to better treatments that work differently from current medicines.

**What’s next step** – Since the study was done in rats and lab cells, the next step is to test if OL is safe and effective in humans. Scientists will need to run clinical trials before it can be used as a medicine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A natural compound called obakulactone may offer a new approach to reduce inflammation and joint damage in rheumatoid arthritis by targeting fat metabolism and immune activity.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un composé naturel prometteur contre les symptômes de la polyarthrite rhumatoïde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La polyarthrite rhumatoïde (PR) est une maladie où le système immunitaire attaque les articulations, provoquant gonflement, douleur et dégâts. Les traitements actuels ne fonctionnent pas toujours bien pour tous et peuvent avoir des effets secondaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié un composé naturel appelé obakulactone (OL), extrait d’une plante, pour voir s’il pouvait aider contre la PR. Ils ont donné différentes doses d’OL à des rats atteints d’arthrite pendant 21 jours. Le traitement a réduit le gonflement des articulations, amélioré les tissus endommagés et équilibré les cellules immunitaires responsables de l’inflammation. OL a aussi corrigé des problèmes liés à certains acides gras impliqués dans la maladie. Les chercheurs ont découvert qu’OL agit en ciblant une protéine appelée ACOT1, importante dans la maladie.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre une nouvelle façon de traiter la PR en agissant sur le métabolisme des graisses et une protéine spécifique. OL a diminué l’inflammation et empêché la croissance de cellules nuisibles dans les articulations. Cela pourrait mener à de meilleurs traitements différents des médicaments actuels.

**Prochaine étape** – Comme l’étude a été faite sur des rats et des cellules en laboratoire, il faut maintenant vérifier si OL est sûr et efficace chez les humains. Des essais cliniques seront nécessaires avant une utilisation médicale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un composé naturel nommé obakulactone pourrait offrir une nouvelle méthode pour réduire l’inflammation et les dégâts articulaires dans la polyarthrite rhumatoïde en ciblant le métabolisme des graisses et l’activité immunitaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Compuesto natural muestra potencial para reducir síntomas de artritis reumatoide
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La artritis reumatoide (AR) es una enfermedad donde el sistema inmunológico ataca las articulaciones, causando hinchazón, dolor y daño. Los tratamientos actuales no siempre funcionan bien para todos y pueden tener efectos secundarios.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron un compuesto natural llamado obakulactona (OL), extraído de una planta, para ver si podía ayudar con la AR. Administraron diferentes dosis de OL a ratas con artritis durante 21 días. El tratamiento redujo la hinchazón de las articulaciones, mejoró los tejidos dañados y equilibró las células inmunes que causan inflamación. OL también ayudó a corregir problemas con ciertas grasas en el cuerpo relacionadas con la AR. Los investigadores descubrieron que OL actúa sobre una proteína llamada ACOT1, que está involucrada en la enfermedad.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de tratar la AR enfocándose en el metabolismo de las grasas y una proteína específica. OL no solo bajó la inflamación, sino que también detuvo el crecimiento de células dañinas en las articulaciones. Esto podría llevar a mejores tratamientos que funcionen de manera diferente a los medicamentos actuales.

**Próximo paso** – Como el estudio se realizó en ratas y células en laboratorio, el siguiente paso es probar si OL es seguro y efectivo en humanos. Se necesitarán ensayos clínicos antes de que pueda usarse como medicamento.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un compuesto natural llamado obakulactona podría ofrecer un nuevo enfoque para reducir la inflamación y el daño articular en la artritis reumatoide al actuar sobre el metabolismo de las grasas y la actividad inmunitaria.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a delicate, glowing molecular structure of obakulactone gently interacting with a stylized, intricate protein complex labeled ACOT1 represented as interlocking geometric shapes, surrounded by flowing, balanced strands symbolizing unsaturated fatty acids in soft natural hues of amber, cream, and muted green, set against a calm abstract background evoking healing and restoration within joint-like organic forms.

articles.title: RNA synthesis and substrate analog inhibition in the CCHFV polymerase
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10913-wRNA synthesis and substrate analog inhibition in the CCHFV polymerase
articles.content: The about 4000-residue L proteins from the Nairoviridae are the largest known viral polymerases, lacking global structural information and promising nucleotide analog (NA) inhibitors. Here we report structures of full-length Nairoviridae Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV) L including a 3.0-Å-resolution polymerase elongation complex that elucidates mechanisms of both early and late elongation stages. Large additions and insertions are found in all three major functional regions that contain the endonuclease, RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRP), and cap-binding domain of CCHFV L, extending RNA binding paths on both sides of RdRP active site and creating interaction networks critical for virus replication as suggested by CCHFV minigenome assay data. NAs with ribose-2′-modifications identical to the hepatitis C drug sofosbuvir are found to specifically and efficiently inhibit CCHFV RdRP through immediate chain termination mechanism. Using sofosbuvir-hepatitis C virus RdRP system as the reference, the potency of these NAs is further demonstrated in competition assays in the presence of corresponding NTPs.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding the structure and function of the Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV) polymerase and identifies nucleotide analog inhibitors that can efficiently inhibit the virus replication. This has broad implications for developing antiviral drugs against a deadly virus, potentially improving public health outcomes globally.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how to block a deadly virus’s key enzyme
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV) is a dangerous virus that causes severe illness. To multiply, it uses a large protein called polymerase, which helps copy its genetic material. Until now, scientists did not fully understand how this polymerase works or how to stop it.

**What happened** – Researchers studied the full structure of the CCHFV polymerase in great detail. They discovered how the polymerase copies the virus’s RNA step by step. They also found that certain drug-like molecules, similar to a medicine used for hepatitis C called sofosbuvir, can block this polymerase. These molecules stop the virus from copying its RNA, which could stop the virus from spreading.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a clear way to stop the virus by targeting its polymerase. Since CCHFV is a deadly virus with no specific treatment, these findings could lead to new medicines that save lives. The study also helps scientists understand how big viral polymerases work, which could aid research on other viruses.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will likely test these nucleotide-like drugs in lab and animal studies to see if they can safely and effectively treat infections caused by CCHFV. This work could also inspire the design of new drugs that target similar viruses.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research reveals how to block the main enzyme of a deadly virus, opening the door to potential antiviral treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment bloquer l’enzyme clé d’un virus mortel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le virus de la fièvre hémorragique de Crimée-Congo (CCHFV) est un virus dangereux qui cause une maladie grave. Pour se multiplier, il utilise une grande protéine appelée polymérase, qui aide à copier son matériel génétique. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas entièrement comment cette polymérase fonctionne ni comment l’arrêter.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié la structure complète de la polymérase du CCHFV en détail. Ils ont découvert comment la polymérase copie l’ARN du virus étape par étape. Ils ont aussi trouvé que certaines molécules semblables à un médicament utilisé contre l’hépatite C, appelé sofosbuvir, peuvent bloquer cette polymérase. Ces molécules empêchent le virus de copier son ARN, ce qui pourrait stopper sa propagation.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une façon claire d’arrêter le virus en ciblant sa polymérase. Comme le CCHFV est un virus mortel sans traitement spécifique, ces résultats pourraient mener à de nouveaux médicaments qui sauvent des vies. L’étude aide aussi les scientifiques à comprendre comment fonctionnent les grandes polymérases virales, ce qui pourrait aider la recherche sur d’autres virus.

**Quelle est la prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement tester ces médicaments similaires aux nucléotides en laboratoire et sur des animaux pour voir s’ils peuvent traiter les infections à CCHFV de façon sûre et efficace. Ce travail pourrait aussi inspirer la création de nouveaux médicaments ciblant des virus similaires.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche révèle comment bloquer l’enzyme principale d’un virus mortel, ouvrant la voie à des traitements antiviraux potentiels.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo bloquear la enzima clave de un virus mortal
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El virus de la fiebre hemorrágica de Crimea-Congo (CCHFV) es un virus peligroso que causa una enfermedad grave. Para multiplicarse, usa una proteína grande llamada polimerasa, que ayuda a copiar su material genético. Hasta ahora, los científicos no entendían completamente cómo funciona esta polimerasa ni cómo detenerla.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron la estructura completa de la polimerasa del CCHFV con mucho detalle. Descubrieron cómo la polimerasa copia el ARN del virus paso a paso. También encontraron que ciertas moléculas parecidas a un medicamento usado para la hepatitis C llamado sofosbuvir pueden bloquear esta polimerasa. Estas moléculas detienen al virus de copiar su ARN, lo que podría detener su propagación.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra una forma clara de detener el virus al atacar su polimerasa. Como el CCHFV es un virus mortal sin tratamiento específico, estos hallazgos podrían llevar a nuevos medicamentos que salven vidas. El estudio también ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo funcionan las grandes polimerasas virales, lo que podría ayudar en la investigación de otros virus.

**Próximo paso** – Probablemente, los científicos probarán estos medicamentos similares a nucleótidos en laboratorios y animales para ver si pueden tratar infecciones por CCHFV de forma segura y efectiva. Este trabajo también podría inspirar el diseño de nuevos medicamentos que ataquen virus similares.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva investigación muestra cómo bloquear la enzima principal de un virus mortal, abriendo la puerta a posibles tratamientos antivirales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate, glowing molecular landscape featuring a large, ornate polymerase enzyme shaped like an elegant, curved bridge with intertwining RNA strands flowing through it, while delicate, jewel-like nucleotide analog molecules resembling softly glowing keys gently bind and halt the RNA path, symbolizing inhibition and precise molecular interaction.

articles.title: More than a gut feeling
articles.summary: Long overlooked, the connection between our gut microbes and brain is yielding new insights into mental health
articles.content:
Always on your mind: gut microbes have close links with the brain

It is the year 2036. A patient struggling with depression and anxiety is referred to her local psychobiotics clinic where the consultant prescribes a course of personalised pills to improve her symptoms. But rather than acting on her own cells, let alone those in her brain, the pills work by administering specific strains of microbes in her gut.

This future vision of doctors tweaking the contents of your digestive tract to improve complex and hard-to-treat psychological disorders might seem far-fetched. But research over the past couple of decades has revealed the far-reaching impact of the ecosystem of bacteria, fungi, viruses and other microbes that lives within us. Perhaps most surprising has been the associations scientists have discovered between the gut microbiome and our mental health. Now they are exploring whether harnessing this intricate ecosystem could help people feel better.

Growing evidence

The notion that your “gut feeling” connects with your psychological experience of the world is well established in popular imagination. But until recently, scientists were deeply sceptical—particularly of the notion that the multitude of microbial cells inside us could have an impact on mental health. “Fifteen years ago, there was sniggering at the back of the seminar room when people presented their research,” says Professor Phil Burnet, a neuroscientist at the University of Oxford, UK.

But the field of microbial neuroscience has accelerated rapidly over the past decade, thanks to an explosion of lab studies and trials in humans that involve microbiota. Some explore live beneficial microbes known as probiotics, others formulations of microbe food, or prebiotics, to fertilise specific bacterial strains. Meanwhile, heat-treated microbes, or postbiotics, are also being developed.

Together, these studies paint a picture of deep and important connections between the 100 trillion or so microbial cells — from as many as 5000 different species — in your digestive tract and your physical and mental health. “How can we use this to maintain wellbeing or treat brain disorders?” asks Burnet. “Within the gut-brain circuit, the gut microbiome is the most accessible component.”

What happens in vagus

The neurons that operate the gut are sometimes referred to as our “second brain”. Known as the enteric nervous system, in humans it contains an estimated 500 million neurons, more than double the number in the spinal cord. It operates autonomously to control digestion but is also in constant bidirectional communication with the brain via the vagus nerve.

This second brain and its connection with the one inside your skull is one way that gut microbes can affect mental health. There are others too: via metabolites they produce passing into the blood and crossing the blood-brain barrier, by impacting gut hormones and by stimulating the immune system.

“The microbiome is endlessly complicated, and what I suspect is that there is more than one mechanism for the clinical effects that we see,” says Dr Richard Day, V.P. Medical Affairs and Clinical Development within R&D at ADM, a company that researches and markets pre-, pro-, and postbiotics. “We’re still on that journey to unpick the mechanisms of action.”

The fact that the gut microbiome is capable of impacting the brain is, however, clear.

Some of the strongest evidence comes from research into mood disorders, with studies showing associations with gut microbiome composition. Now, scientists are moving beyond these observational studies to experimental interventions to find out more.

A mechanistic study led by the University of Oxford, for example, found that giving participants a probiotic had measurable effects on their cognitive and emotional processing. Other studies are exploring the gut-brain axis in clinical populations. In 2023, for example, researchers at King’s College London and ADM published a study in JAMA Psychiatry, exploring the effects of a probiotic in people with major depression. “Together, these studies paint an increasingly compelling picture of the gut-brain connection,” says Dr Malwina Naghibi, Head of Clinical Development at ADM.

A two-way street

This connection works both ways: mental health can affect digestive health and vice versa. Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), for example, has an established relationship with anxiety, and this link is being actively explored. In one study, ADM’s researchers gave adults with IBS either a live or a heat-treated strain of gut bacteria. Both produced similar results in patients, providing researchers with vital clues on how bacteria—alive or dead—interact with the gut and nervous system (see Box, right).

scientists are relishing the challenge of exploring this new frontier

These studies are typical of a proliferation of mostly small, but rigorous clinical trials testing the impact of the gut microbiome on depression and anxiety, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and stress. There is even early research into whether it plays a role in Alzheimer’s disease.

What’s needed now are larger studies over longer periods that can show how robust any impacts really are. “There is just this explosion of research at the moment,” says Naghibi.

The sniggering at the back of seminar rooms has gone quiet, but there is still a long way to go. Microbial neuroscience is a field in its infancy, psychobiotics even more so. Yet scientists are relishing the challenge of exploring this new frontier. “I think this is the most exciting part,” says Naghibi.

MICROBIAL AFTERLIVES—THE SCIENCE OF POSTBIOTICS The new kid on the block in microbiome research is postbiotics. Unlike probiotics (live microbes) or prebiotics (microbe nutrients), these are preparations of microbes inactivated by heat or other methods, sometimes also together with the soup of cell fragments and metabolites they produce (see graphic). Even though the microbes are no longer alive, they can still exert biological effects. ADM scientists, for example, have shown that heat-treated bacteria produce similar effects to live ones in people who have IBS. These effects can be rapid. A recent study of postbiotics and the gut-brain axis noted physiological changes within an hour of administration. And findings from other trials suggest that the effects of postbiotics wear off when participants stop taking them. This suggests a direct effect rather than a permanent change to the microbiota, though much more research on mechanisms is needed. Meanwhile, postbiotics are being investigated in a wide range of conditions. The ADM team, for example, has conducted a trial of 72 people exploring whether postbiotics can reduce the symptoms of allergic rhinitis.

The advantage of postbiotics over probiotics is that they don’t need to be kept alive, meaning they can be added to foods or drinks. Why swallow a pill if you could take postbiotics in the milk on your cereal?

Learn more at: https://www.adm.com/en-us/products-services/human-nutrition/products/microbiome-solutions/
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant scientific progress in understanding and potentially treating mental health disorders through the gut microbiome, a breakthrough with broad implications for millions of people worldwide. It provides detailed context on the emerging field of microbial neuroscience, clinical trials, and innovative treatments like probiotics and postbiotics, highlighting a hopeful future for mental health therapies.
articles.personality_title: New discoveries show gut microbes affect mental health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied bacteria in the gut mostly for digestion. Recently, they found that the microbes living in our digestive system also influence our brain and mental health.

**What happened** – Over the past 20 years, research has grown fast on how gut microbes connect to the brain. Studies show that helpful bacteria, called probiotics, and other treatments like prebiotics (food for microbes) and postbiotics (inactivated microbes) can change mood and emotions. For example, a 2023 study gave people with depression probiotics and saw improvements. The gut and brain communicate through the nervous system and chemicals made by microbes.

**Impact** – This is important because it opens new ways to treat mental health problems like depression, anxiety, and even conditions like irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) that affect both gut and mind. Unlike some medicines, these treatments work by changing the bacteria in the gut, which is easier to reach than the brain itself. Postbiotics have the advantage of being easy to add to everyday foods.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to run bigger and longer studies to confirm how well these treatments work. They also want to understand exactly how microbes affect the brain. This knowledge could lead to new, personalized therapies that help many people with mental health disorders.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are uncovering how gut microbes influence the brain, offering new hope for better mental health treatments through the digestive system.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles découvertes montrent que les microbes intestinaux influencent la santé mentale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudiaient autrefois les bactéries intestinales surtout pour la digestion. Récemment, ils ont découvert que les microbes vivant dans notre système digestif influencent aussi notre cerveau et notre santé mentale.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Au cours des 20 dernières années, la recherche s'est accélérée sur la connexion entre microbes intestinaux et cerveau. Des études montrent que des bactéries bénéfiques, appelées probiotiques, et d'autres traitements comme les prébiotiques (nourriture pour microbes) et les postbiotiques (microbes inactivés) peuvent modifier l'humeur et les émotions. Par exemple, une étude de 2023 a donné des probiotiques à des personnes dépressives et a observé des améliorations. L'intestin et le cerveau communiquent via le système nerveux et des substances chimiques produites par les microbes.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela ouvre de nouvelles façons de traiter des problèmes de santé mentale comme la dépression, l’anxiété, et même des conditions comme le syndrome de l’intestin irritable (SII) qui affectent à la fois l’intestin et l’esprit. Contrairement à certains médicaments, ces traitements agissent en modifiant les bactéries dans l’intestin, plus facile à atteindre que le cerveau lui-même. Les postbiotiques ont l’avantage de pouvoir être facilement ajoutés aux aliments quotidiens.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent réaliser des études plus larges et plus longues pour confirmer l’efficacité de ces traitements. Ils veulent aussi comprendre précisément comment les microbes influencent le cerveau. Ces connaissances pourraient mener à de nouvelles thérapies personnalisées aidant beaucoup de personnes souffrant de troubles mentaux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques découvrent comment les microbes intestinaux influencent le cerveau, offrant de nouvelles pistes pour améliorer les traitements de la santé mentale via le système digestif.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos descubrimientos muestran que los microbios intestinales afectan la salud mental
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos solían estudiar las bacterias del intestino principalmente para la digestión. Recientemente, descubrieron que los microbios que viven en nuestro sistema digestivo también influyen en el cerebro y la salud mental.

**Qué pasó** – En los últimos 20 años, la investigación sobre la conexión entre microbios intestinales y cerebro ha crecido rápidamente. Estudios muestran que bacterias beneficiosas llamadas probióticos, y otros tratamientos como prebióticos (alimento para microbios) y postbióticos (microbios inactivados) pueden cambiar el estado de ánimo y las emociones. Por ejemplo, un estudio de 2023 dio probióticos a personas con depresión y vio mejoras. El intestino y el cerebro se comunican a través del sistema nervioso y sustancias químicas producidas por los microbios.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque abre nuevas formas de tratar problemas de salud mental como depresión, ansiedad, e incluso condiciones como el síndrome del intestino irritable (SII) que afectan al intestino y la mente. A diferencia de algunos medicamentos, estos tratamientos funcionan al cambiar las bacterias en el intestino, que es más fácil de alcanzar que el cerebro. Los postbióticos tienen la ventaja de poder añadirse fácilmente a alimentos comunes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren hacer estudios más grandes y largos para confirmar qué tan bien funcionan estos tratamientos. También quieren entender exactamente cómo los microbios afectan el cerebro. Este conocimiento podría llevar a nuevas terapias personalizadas que ayuden a muchas personas con trastornos mentales.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos están descubriendo cómo los microbios intestinales influyen en el cerebro, ofreciendo nuevas esperanzas para mejores tratamientos de salud mental a través del sistema digestivo.

articles.title: Short HIIT sessions can offset sleep debt’s metabolic toll
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Most people treat sleep debt as a tiredness problem. Get some extra rest on the weekend, recover, move on. The reality is messier. Missing sleep for several nights triggers a cascade of metabolic and cardiovascular changes that don’t reverse cleanly when you finally catch up. Research is pointing to […]
The post Short HIIT sessions can offset sleep debt’s metabolic toll first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Most people treat sleep debt as a tiredness problem. Get some extra rest on the weekend, recover, move on. The reality is messier. Missing sleep for several nights triggers a cascade of metabolic and cardiovascular changes that don’t reverse cleanly when you finally catch up. Research is pointing to a specific type of short, intense exercise as one of the more effective partial countermeasures.

Why sleep debt compounds the way it does

Missing an hour of sleep a night for five nights leaves a five-hour deficit by the weekend. The problem is your circadian rhythm. “Our ability to sleep is not just determined by our homeostatic sleep drive, or how tired you feel based on how long you’ve been awake,” explains Jennifer Martin, PhD, spokesperson for the American Academy of Sleep Medicine and professor at Florida International University. “It’s also governed by our circadian rhythm, which will not just allow us to sleep five hours later than normal to account for that debt.”

In other words, your internal clock will push you awake at your usual time regardless of what you owe your body. And even when you do manage extra sleep, it “will not fully erase the impact on the body of days of insufficient sleep,” says Angela Holliday-Bell, MD, a board-certified physician and sleep specialist.

The downstream effects go well beyond tiredness. Sleep debt raises inflammation and stress hormones, adding strain to the heart and blood vessels and increasing risk of high blood pressure over time. It also dulls insulin sensitivity, the mechanism by which cells absorb glucose from the bloodstream, raising blood sugar levels and long-term risk of type 2 diabetes. At the brain level, the disruption compounds further. Impaired glucose uptake makes it harder for neurons to produce the energy they need to function, says Louisa Nicola, MMed, a neurophysiologist who studies Alzheimer’s disease. Sustained over time, this reduces the brain’s resilience against aging and illness.

Where high-intensity exercise comes in

Short sessions of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) appear to partially offset some of this damage. Several recent studies comparing people who did HIIT during or after periods of sleep restriction against those who did not found that the HIIT groups showed better metabolic outcomes, including less insulin resistance.

This fits with what research already established about HIIT: sessions of 10 to 15 minutes produce measurable improvements in blood sugar control, disproportionate to their length. Counteracting the insulin sensitivity drop from sleep debt also benefits the brain, Nicola points out, since better glucose uptake means more available energy for cognition.

HIIT also increases blood flow to the brain and triggers the release of brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), a protein associated with neural health. Some studies suggest doing HIIT before a night of restricted sleep can reduce the negative impact on memory, with better recall than in sleep-deprived people who did not work out. “Vigorous exercise like HIIT enhances blood flow to your brain and triggers the release of chemicals like BDNF,” says Holliday-Bell. “All of which can support cognition.”

What HIIT does not do

HIIT helps with the metabolic fallout of sleep debt. It does not cancel it.

If you are significantly sleep-deprived, injury risk during intense exercise goes up. And your body needs adequate sleep after HIIT to resolve the temporary inflammation and muscle micro-tears the workout produces, and ongoing sleep deprivation during a HIIT routine undercuts the benefit you are trying to get.

Dr. Martin’s practical advice: stop trying to bank extra sleep on weekends or run a strategic deficit. Aim to meet your baseline sleep need consistently instead. For most adults, that is seven to nine hours a night. To find your personal number, Martin suggests noting how long you sleep on the third day of a vacation, after the initial rebound from fatigue has settled and with no obligations forcing an early wake-up. That figure tends to reflect your real baseline.

When sleep debt is unavoidable, a short HIIT session is a meaningful buffer. It is not a workaround for sleep itself, and nothing else is either.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on research showing that short HIIT exercise sessions can partially offset the negative metabolic and cognitive effects of sleep debt, offering a practical, evidence-based strategy to improve health outcomes for a broad population facing common sleep challenges. It provides detailed scientific context and actionable advice, making it a meaningful health breakthrough with wide relevance.
articles.personality_title: Short HIIT workouts can help reduce health risks from lost sleep
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people lose sleep during the week and try to catch up on weekends. But missing several nights of sleep causes changes in the body that extra rest alone does not fully fix. These changes increase health risks like high blood pressure and diabetes.

**What happened** – Recent research shows that short sessions of high-intensity interval training (HIIT), lasting 10 to 15 minutes, can partly counteract the harmful effects of sleep loss. People who exercised with HIIT during or after sleep restriction had better blood sugar control and brain function than those who did not.

**Impact** – This is important because sleep debt affects how the body controls blood sugar and stresses the heart. HIIT helps improve insulin sensitivity, allowing cells to absorb glucose better, which supports both physical health and brain energy. It also increases blood flow to the brain and releases proteins that support memory and brain health.

**What's next step** – Experts say HIIT is helpful but not a replacement for regular sleep. The best approach is to get enough sleep every night—usually seven to nine hours. When missing sleep is unavoidable, adding short HIIT workouts can reduce some of the risks. Future research may explore how to combine sleep and exercise habits for better health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Short, intense exercise sessions can partly protect the body and brain from the harmful effects of not getting enough sleep, but regular sleep remains essential.
articles.personality_title_fr: De courtes séances de HIIT peuvent réduire les risques liés au manque de sommeil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes manquent de sommeil durant la semaine et essaient de récupérer le week-end. Mais plusieurs nuits de sommeil insuffisant provoquent des changements dans le corps que le repos supplémentaire ne corrige pas complètement. Ces changements augmentent les risques pour la santé comme l’hypertension et le diabète.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des recherches récentes montrent que de courtes séances d’entraînement par intervalles à haute intensité (HIIT), de 10 à 15 minutes, peuvent en partie contrebalancer les effets négatifs du manque de sommeil. Les personnes qui ont fait du HIIT pendant ou après une période de privation de sommeil ont eu un meilleur contrôle de leur glycémie et une meilleure fonction cérébrale que celles qui n’ont pas fait d’exercice.

**Impact** – Cela est important car la dette de sommeil affecte la gestion du sucre dans le sang et met le cœur sous pression. Le HIIT améliore la sensibilité à l’insuline, ce qui aide les cellules à mieux absorber le glucose, soutenant ainsi la santé physique et l’énergie du cerveau. Il augmente aussi le flux sanguin vers le cerveau et libère des protéines qui aident la mémoire et la santé cérébrale.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts recommandent le HIIT comme aide, mais pas comme substitut au sommeil régulier. La meilleure solution reste de dormir suffisamment chaque nuit, généralement entre sept et neuf heures. Quand le manque de sommeil est inévitable, ajouter des séances courtes de HIIT peut réduire certains risques. Les recherches futures pourraient explorer comment combiner sommeil et exercice pour une meilleure santé.

**En une phrase** – De courtes séances d’exercice intense peuvent en partie protéger le corps et le cerveau des effets négatifs du manque de sommeil, mais un sommeil régulier reste essentiel.
articles.personality_title_es: Sesiones cortas de HIIT pueden reducir riesgos por falta de sueño
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas pierden sueño durante la semana y tratan de recuperarlo el fin de semana. Pero faltar varias noches de sueño provoca cambios en el cuerpo que el descanso extra no corrige completamente. Estos cambios aumentan riesgos para la salud como la presión alta y la diabetes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigaciones recientes muestran que sesiones cortas de entrenamiento por intervalos de alta intensidad (HIIT), de 10 a 15 minutos, pueden contrarrestar en parte los efectos negativos de la falta de sueño. Las personas que hicieron HIIT durante o después de periodos de sueño restringido tuvieron mejor control del azúcar en sangre y función cerebral que quienes no hicieron ejercicio.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la deuda de sueño afecta cómo el cuerpo controla el azúcar en la sangre y pone estrés en el corazón. El HIIT mejora la sensibilidad a la insulina, ayudando a las células a absorber mejor la glucosa, lo que apoya la salud física y la energía cerebral. También aumenta el flujo sanguíneo al cerebro y libera proteínas que ayudan a la memoria y salud cerebral.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos dicen que el HIIT es útil pero no reemplaza el sueño regular. Lo mejor es dormir lo suficiente cada noche, generalmente entre siete y nueve horas. Cuando no se puede dormir lo suficiente, agregar sesiones cortas de HIIT puede reducir algunos riesgos. Investigaciones futuras podrían combinar hábitos de sueño y ejercicio para mejorar la salud.

**Resumen en una frase** – Sesiones cortas de ejercicio intenso pueden proteger en parte al cuerpo y cerebro de los efectos dañinos de la falta de sueño, pero dormir bien sigue siendo esencial.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant heart and brain interconnected by flowing streams of glowing energy, with a small, dynamic silhouette of a runner mid-stride symbolizing short, intense bursts of exercise, all rendered in soft natural tones of warm reds, gentle blues, and earthy neutrals.
